title: Menopausal Therapy: Is Timing Everything? with Dr. JoAnn Manson
text:
[Music]
[Music]
so our topic today is hormone
replacement therapy and despite the fact
that menopause can have such a profound
effect on the lives and health of women
and that the risks and benefits of HRT
have never been more clear the use of
systemic hormone therapy as Dr Manson
mentioned in one of her articles has
decreased by 80% in the past 20 years
medical trainees are not adequately
educated in the proper use of H HRT I
can attest to that uh and as she also
noted women's decisions around the use
of HRT is now mired in anxiety and
confusion so to clear up that confusion
for us tonight is one of the world's
most preeminent clinician scientists
and a renowned expert in this particular
topic and that's Dr Joanne Manson Dr
Manson is a professor of medicine and
the Michael and Lee Bell professor of
women's health at Harvard Medical School
she's also a professor in the department
of epidemiology at the Harvard th Chan
School of Public Health she's chief of
the division of preventive medicine at
brigam and Women's Hospital and she's
also there the scientific adviser to
their Center for Women's Health and sex
and gender
Medicine Dr Manson is a physician
epidemiologist and endocrinologist and
the pi or coopi of a range of research
studies quite remarkable actually
including the Women's Health Initiative
in the clinical Center in Boston the
cardiovascular component of the nurse's
health study uh the vitamin D and
omega-3 trial otherwise known as vital
the Coco supplement and multivitamin oom
study otherwise known as cos Cosmos and
the vitamin D for covid-19 or Vivid
trial obviously her her primary research
interests include randomized clinical
prevention Trials of nutritional and
lifestyle factors related to heart
disease diabetes and cancer and the role
of endogenous and exogenous estrogens as
determinants of chronic disease her H
index is 303 and just to put that in
perspective you know the average H index
of a full professor in the United States
is about 12 to 24 an outstanding
scientist is around 60 and a truly
unique unicorn scientist is around 90 so
that just puts in PP by the way I by the
way I've never met anyone with an index
H index of 303 either not even close so
Dr Manson really thank you very much for
taking the time to be with us today um
we're all very much looking forward to
your presentation and your words of
wisdom around this very important
topic well thank you so much David for
that very very kind
introduction and uh
as David mentioned there's so much
confusion around hormone therapy I hope
that I can help clarify some of the
issues related to estrogen therapy and
estrogen plus progestin therapy I would
like to review for you um some of the
recent findings from randomized clinical
trials and their implications for
clinical
practice I have no Financial conflicts
of interest related to the present
ation now as an obscure historical
figure once said um things should be as
simple as possible but not any simpler
and I think the Epic Saga of hormone
therapy is really one of the best
examples of this the problem has been
trying to make things too simple a a
one-size fits-all answer for all women
hormone therapy is either good for all
women or it's bad for all women we
started out thinking hormone therapy
should be given routinely to all women
um before the Women's Health Initiative
it was one of the most commonly
prescribed medications then after the
Women's Health Initiative the as David
mentioned there there was a reduction of
80% in uh the prescriptions for hormone
therapy and and clearly both approaches
are not appropriate hormone therapy
should not be used for all women and it
should not be denied to all women there
are some women who are very good
candidates um and it's really important
that clinicians um understand the
research and how to identify the women
who are good candidates for hormone
therapy so that's what I hope to do uh
today so the goals of the talk are to
review recent findings from the Whi and
other randomized trials of hormone
therapy describe some of the uh clinical
characteristics that are important in
Risk stratification and deciding who's a
good candidate for hormone therapy and
this includes age time since menopause
underlying clinical risk factors whether
a woman has metabolic syndrome whether
she has
dyslipidemia blood pressure Etc some of
the biomarkers that have been helpful in
Risk
ratification and all of these variables
how do they modify our health outcomes
on hormone therapy and how can they be
used in identifying women who are
particularly good candidates for hormone
therapy and those who should avoid
hormone therapy so I also um want to go
through some of the clinical
decisionmaking algorith ithms that are
available and address the role of recent
research in improving um clinical
decision making for um hormonal therapy
and some of the options that are
available for non hormonal
therapy now this um prospective piece
was circulated it was uh published in
New England Journal several years ago
and it was to make the case that um
women are receiving very fragmented care
in terms of menopause most clinicians
have decided just not to prescribe
hormone therapy it's too complicated and
um many women are suffering because of
that they have severe moderate to severe
hot flashes night sweats often
interrupting sleep um impairing quality
of life and they're having a lot of
trouble finding clinicians to treat them
and I actually hear from a lot of these
women I try to help them to find
clinicians in their area the North
American menopause Society at
menopause.org they actually have a
website where you can have um patients
search um for according to their zip
code for clinicians who have special
training in Men in menopause management
so I've often referred women in other
parts of the country um to that website
but this is a a real issue and um about
75% of women in the per menopause or the
early menopause will have um vasomotor
symptoms hot flashes night sweats about
20% of women will have what's classified
as moderate to severe symptoms and would
be uh would be candidates for menopausal
hormone therapy not all women with hot
flashes night sweats need medication
many can be treated with lifestyle
modifications and avoiding triggers um
or the symptoms are so mild that it
isn't really um worth going on hormone
therapy but about 20% will have uh
significant symptoms that disrupt sleep
and impair quality of life and hormone
therapy is the most effective treatment
for vasomotor
symptoms now this slide just shows how
um menopausal therapy reduces the number
of hot flashes in in the trials that
have been done one a finding to point
out is that you always need a placebo
group in these randomized trials of any
treatment for hot flashes basom motor
symptoms because even in the placebo
group shown in white you're going to see
a 30 40% reduction but with menopausal
estrogen therapy this is uh conjugated
equin estrogens plus medroxy
progesterone itate even with very low
doses the traditional dose is
625 milligrams of conjugated equin
estrogens what we tested in the women's
health initiative and about 2.5
milligrams of mroy progesterone acetate
or MPA this is not to recommend this
regimen we'll get into some of those
formulation issues in a minute but it's
just to show whether you use the
traditional dose or lower dose es even
down to
0.3 milligrams of um conjugated equin
estrogens you are going to see an 80 to
85% reduction in hot flashes night
sweats very substantial reductions um
that are clearly separated from the
effect seen in Placebo now some women
are not candidates for hormone therapy
we we'll talk about which ones in
particular should avoid hormone therapy
and fortunately
over the last 10 15 years there are some
non hormonal treatments that are
available so um we have the lowd dose
proxi tee which which actually is the
only FDA approved treatment but there
are many other forms of anti-depressants
there's the S ssris the
snris um the serotonin and
norepinephrine reuptake Inhibitors such
as venlafaxine
ocol these are all all at least
moderately effective 40 50 uh 30 to 50%
reduction compared to
Placebo not quite as effective as
menopausal hormone therapy but still can
make enough of a difference that they
are are really worth trying in a woman
who is not a candidate for estrogen
therapy
Gabapentin um is another category of
treatments these are the most common
doses of the non hormonal and uh that
also has been associated with about 40
to 50% reduction in vasomotor symptoms
so these are important to keep in mind
um as backups in women who who are not
candidates for menopausal hormone
therapy but generally the societies of
women's health North American menopause
Society endocrine Society um American
Association of clinical endocrinologists
many others do consider consider
menopausal estrogen therapy or
combination estrogen progestin therapy
as the first line of
treatment now I want to talk about why
the effects of hormone therapy may
actually differ um in younger women
early menopausal women versus uh women
more remote from menopause and um the
here are two cross sections of coronary
arteries one in um early menopause and
one um women in later menopause and um
in in the one on the right this is in
the context of established
atherosclerosis now on the left in a
woman who is very recently menopausal
she's likely to have healthy coronary
arteries of course she'll have some
fatty streaks but she is not likely to
have complicated plaque and her
endothelium is likely to be very
responsive to estrogen what estrogen
does is it increases nitric oxide
synthesis and that leads to vasod
dilation in a healthy uh blood vessel
where the endothelium is normal as in
early menopause it also has the effect
of actually reducing um inflammatory
activation luccy adhesion some of the
lipid changes can decrease progression
of atherosclerotic plaque I'll show you
some evidence for that as well so it's
an overall quite favorable effect of
exogenous estrogen on the endothelium
and the coronary artery in a woman in
early menopause now in sharp contrast in
a woman let's say in her later 60s and
70s who may have um Advanced plaques
estrogen has almost the opposite effect
first the endothelium is not accessible
to the estrogen the The receptors are
underneath fibrous cap they're they're
they're really not
accessible and estrogen does not have
the effect of increasing nitric oxide
synthesis or leading to Vaso dilation in
fact there's some evidence it may lead
to a paradoxical
vasoconstriction in the setting of a
stenotic blood vessel where there's all
already advanced plaque another issue as
you well know is that you can have a
stenotic blood vessel that may be
particularly vulnerable to um blockage
from um from a clot especially with oral
estrogen which increases coagulation
factors inre is clotting in that
scenario a an older woman who has a
stenotic blood vessel she may be at high
risk not only of a ruptured or eroded
plaque due to you know some of the
effects of estrogen on MMP and she will
not have the vasod dilation but she may
actually have thrombo occlusion of the
blood vessel so two directly opposite
effect effects on the coronary arteries
when estrogen is given in early
menopause versus later menopause so it's
not too surprising that the results may
end up differing by by age and time
since
menopause but for so many decades we did
not have randomized clinical trials we
had the observational studies and the
observational studies based on more than
40 uh fairly large studies in including
you know nurses health study the summary
relative risk for uh coronary disease
and women who were using hormone therapy
were about 40 to 50% lower than in women
who never used hormone therapy very
marked different differences and of
course some of this um Association may
have been related to confounding that
women who were being prescribed hormone
therapy tended to be higher
socioeconomic status better access to
health care probably Health more health
conscious healthier lifestyle practices
but it's also the case that in the
observational studies many of the women
seeking hormone therapy were seeking
treatment for hot flashes night sweats
and they were in early menopause um
compared to in the randomized trial many
of the women are at older ages they they
they don't have severe menopausal
symptoms so there's a very different
clinical group in the observational
studies and the randomized trials but
the observational studies actually did
not really uh reveal that there were
major differences by age group and that
may be because the women even if they
were in their their 60s or 70s
had been taking hormone therapy since
early menopause and they were just
continuing to take the hormone therapy
or their overall risk of coronary
disease and general cardiovascular
disease was so low due to other factors
such as healthier lifestyle practices
and um a higher socioeconomic status but
the these age differences time since
menopause differences really did not be
come fully apparent until more recent
analyses of randomized
Trials now this slide just summarizes
some of the differences between the
Women's Health Initiative we're going to
talk about in some detail where the
average age at hormone therapy
initiation was 63 years the women were
50 to
79 versus the observational studies
where the average age at HT initiation
was 52 years
the time since menopause obviously be
quite different under that scenario more
than 12 years um in the Whi and only one
to you know three years in observational
studies and you may be wondering well
why in the world did Whi test women with
an average age of 63 years the whole
spectrum of Ages 50 to 79 and 12 or more
years since menopause on average well
there was a
very strong rationale for that at the
time the Whi was started it wasn't clear
that there were differences by age or
time since menopause the purpose of the
Whi was not to look at the efficacy of
hormone therapy for treating hot flashes
it was to assess the balance of benefits
and risks of hormone therapy for chronic
disease outcomes such as heart disease
stroke Cancer all cause mortality and if
you study women at the very beginning of
menopause you're going to need a study
about 30 years
long um so it was really important to
have women who were um covering the
Spectrum um all the way into their 70s
as well and had an you know this average
age of 63
years now in the late
1990s early 2000s some of the secondary
prevention Tri Trials of women's of of
hormone therapy began to emerge um
probably the best known is the heart and
estrogen progest replacement study or
hers that was published in 1998 and this
was a study again of conjugated equin
estrogen and MPA looking at women with a
history of cardiovascular disease and it
showed not only no benefit for reducing
coronary events or other cardiovascular
events it actually showed a slight
increased risk during year one which may
be due to what I was showing you um The
increased risk of plaque rupture maybe
of a thrombo eclusive event in a
stenotic blood vessel these other um
randomized trials I don't have time to
go through them in detail but it's not
necessary because I can summarize in one
sentence that not a single one of the
secondary prevention trials showed a
benefit of hormone therapy for clinical
cardiovascular events in a secondary
prevention setting so when you take
women who are already at high risk of
cardiovascular disease they're postm or
they have let's say documented
atherosclerosis or prior stroke any of
these um high-risk women they do not do
well with hormone therapy and it really
should not be prescribed in that uh
scenario and this is true whether it's
oral estrogen or transdermal estrogen
whether it's um which regardless of the
formulation of the progestogen it is
really not a a favorable setting for
initiating hormone therapy use so this
brings us to the two large scale
randomized trials in primary prevention
the parallel trials estrogen plus
progestin and estrogen alone trial in
the women's health initi
now um the design of this trial again it
was women ages um 50 uh 50 to 79 I'm I'm
going to just get rid of this floating
bar at the top um and women it was
divided up into whether or not women had
prior hysterectomy so over 10,000 women
had prior hysterectomy and they could be
randomized to estrogen without a
progestin versus
placebo and um then the women who had an
intact uterus more than
16,000 they need to take a progestin to
to uh protect the uterus and they were
randomized to continuous combined
conjugated equine estrogen plus MPA at
uh 2.5 milligrams a day again it was the

now the results of the Whi the e+p trial
first are shown in this slide on this
slide now clearly this is a very
unfavorable benefit risk ratio and this
is why the um hormone therapy e+ P trial
which was slated to last eight years
actually stopped 3.3 years early um
there was an increased risk of coronary
heart disease
stroke pulmonary embolism even breast
cancer though the breast cancer risk
with e+ P didn't emerge until about four
years after a randomization there were
some outcomes on the benefit side there
was about onethird reduction in hip

it was
clear that the risks outweighed the

less so with e alone but uh the the the

findings in
2002 led to this 80% reduction in

end points was uh
neutral now that's where um everything

this the cross-section slide I was

Placebo was

with intact
uterus now I want to show you some
